title: De-Positioning
subtitle: The Secret Brand Strategy for Creating Competitive Advantage
author: Todd Irwin
keyTakeaways: Traditional positioning no longer works in saturated markets — de-position competitors instead
Find the single 'Hero Pain Point' customers care most about
Own one idea in the customer's mind — focus creates power
Make the customer the hero, not your brand
Align the entire organization around the one idea
overview: Traditional brand positioning — trying to differentiate by claiming uniqueness — no longer works in saturated markets. Instead, brands should de-position competitors by identifying real customer pain points that rivals can't or won't solve, then aligning the entire organization around eliminating that pain.
overviewQuote: Differentiation alone is not enough. Brands win not by shouting louder, but by solving the one problem competitors leave unresolved — making alternatives feel unnecessary.

principles.title: Abandon the Search for "White Space"
principles.description: Every category is already occupied. Instead of inventing a new lane, redefine existing space by reframing how customers see current options. Identify a clear "enemy" — not a competitor, but a problem or outdated way of doing things.

principles.title: Find the "Hero Pain Point"
principles.description: Not one of many problems — the single most emotionally charged and practically urgent issue customers want solved. Focus creates power. Trying to address too many needs weakens impact.

principles.title: Make the Customer the Hero
principles.description: Customers don't wake up hoping to admire a brand. They wake up because something isn't working. Winning brands identify the obstacle and organize everything around removing it.

principles.title: Study Competitors to Expose What They Can't Fix
principles.description: Study rivals deeply — not to copy, but to uncover what they cannot or will not fix. Every organization carries limitations. Those weaknesses are your strategic openings.

principles.title: Own One Idea in the Customer's Mind
principles.description: Brands that try to stand for too many things end up standing for nothing. One strong concept creates a mental shortcut — easy to understand, easy to remember.

principles.title: Integrate Across the Entire Organization
principles.description: If the external promise doesn't match the internal experience, trust collapses. Every part of the business must reinforce the same strategic idea.

frameworks.name: The De-Positioning Process
steps: Research & Observe — Deep customer research + competitor analysis
Identify the Hero Pain Point — The single most urgent, unresolved customer frustration
Map Competitor Weaknesses — Where do rivals fail to solve this pain?
Define Your One Idea — The clear, ownable solution that makes alternatives feel outdated
Align the Organization — Every touchpoint reinforces the one idea
Execute with Discipline — Say no to anything that dilutes focus

table.description: Not a competitor — a problem, inefficiency, or outdated approach
The hero pain point customers feel most urgently
How your solution makes the old way feel inadequate

caseStudies.name: Apple — The Second-Mover Masterclass
caseStudies.description: Didn't succeed by being first. Observed early players, let them introduce complexity and frustration, then entered with simplicity, integration, and design. De-positioned competitors as complicated and uninspiring.

caseStudies.name: Volvo — Owning One Idea for Decades
caseStudies.description: Owned safety. Didn't try to be fastest, most luxurious, or most affordable. Every decision reinforced one concept. Discipline in owning one idea creates lasting brand equity.

b2bApplications.application: Map the #1 frustration for each audience segment. Own the one that matters most.
Lead with their pain, not our capabilities. Center the customer's problem everywhere.
Audit competitor CX — slow onboarding? Opaque pricing? Bad support? Those gaps are our strategy.
One promise per audience. Brokers: speed. Employers: certainty. TPAs: integration.
CX promises mean nothing if sales, onboarding, and support don't deliver the same experience.
Study what incumbents get wrong. Build the experience that makes their way feel outdated.

quotes: Brands win not by being different, but by being the clear antidote to an unresolved frustration.
Focus creates power. One strong concept creates a mental shortcut.
If the promise a brand makes externally does not match the experience delivered internally, trust collapses.
Customers don't wake up hoping to admire a brand's personality. They wake up because something isn't working.
actionItems: Run a Hero Pain Point exercise for each audience segment
Audit 3-5 competitor websites and CX flows
Define our "One Idea" per audience and pressure-test it
Align website copy, sales materials, and support scripts around that idea
Create an internal alignment check — does every team reinforce the promise?

title: B2B Customer Experience — Deep Dive
subtitle: Multi-Source Research
author: Multi-source research
keyTakeaways: CX is the differentiator — not price, not features
Reduce effort, don't delight — make it easy, not impressive
B2B buyers are anxious humans — address the emotional job
Self-service is expected — build for independence first
Post-purchase is where revenue lives — invest in retention
Align internally or fail externally — disconnected teams = disconnected CX
overview: B2B CX is how a business customer perceives your brand across every interaction — from first research to onboarding, ongoing service, and renewal. Unlike B2C, it involves multiple stakeholders, longer sales cycles, higher stakes, and ongoing relationships.
overviewQuote: B2B buyers are still humans. They bring the same expectations from their B2C experiences into professional purchasing. The gap between B2C ease and B2B friction is where trust breaks.

principles.title: Reduce Effort, Don't Delight
principles.description: Loyalty comes from reducing effort, not exceeding expectations. Customers don't reward you for going above and beyond — they punish you for making things hard.

principles.title: Self-Service Is the New Default
principles.description: 65–81% of B2B buyers prefer to research and solve problems independently. Winning companies build better self-service tools, not bigger sales teams.

principles.title: The Buying Committee Challenge
principles.description: B2B purchases involve 3-10 people with different priorities: economic buyers care about cost/ROI, technical buyers about integration/security, user buyers about ease of use, champions about career advancement, and blockers about risk avoidance.

principles.title: Post-Purchase Is Where the Money Is
principles.description: In recurring revenue models, 80%+ of revenue comes after the initial sale. Yet most companies spend 80%+ of CX effort on acquisition.

principles.title: Internal Alignment Is Non-Negotiable
principles.description: Only 24% of service leaders have full-funnel visibility. The biggest CX failures aren't bad interactions — they're disconnected interactions.

principles.title: Emotional Experience Matters More Than You Think
principles.description: Anxiety drives B2B decisions more than logic. Buyers choose vendors who make them feel safe and confident. Trust is the currency.

frameworks.name: The Trust Equation
frameworks.description: Trust = (Credibility + Reliability + Intimacy) ÷ Self-Orientation. Every touchpoint builds or erodes trust. The biggest killer is self-orientation — talking about your features instead of the customer's problem.

frameworks.name: Jobs to Be Done (JTBD) for B2B

table.description: "Help me feel confident I made the right choice"
"Help me look competent to my leadership"

frameworks.note: The emotional and social jobs are often more important than the functional job — but most B2B companies only address functional needs.

frameworks.name: Buyer Anxiety Model
steps: Pre-purchase anxiety: "Am I choosing the right vendor? Will I look bad if this fails?"
Purchase anxiety: "Is this contract fair? Am I missing something?"
Post-purchase anxiety: "Did I make the right call? Is this working?"
frameworks.note: CX must address all three phases. Most companies only focus on pre-purchase and ignore post-purchase — where churn happens.

stats.description: of organizations expect to compete mainly on CX
of B2B pros prefer vendors offering self-service tools
of B2B buyers purchased without a single sales meeting
of customers expect ticket resolution within 3 hours
of B2B buyers say the buying experience is outdated

quotes: B2B buyers are still humans. They bring the same expectations from their B2C experiences into professional purchasing.
Loyalty comes from reducing effort, not exceeding expectations.
In recurring revenue models, 80%+ of revenue comes after the initial sale.
Anxiety drives B2B decisions more than logic. Buyers choose vendors who make them feel safe and confident.
actionItems: CX is the differentiator — not price, not features
Reduce effort, don't delight — make it easy, not impressive
B2B buyers are anxious humans — address the emotional job
Self-service is expected — build for independence first
Post-purchase is where revenue lives — invest in retention
Align internally or fail externally — disconnected teams = disconnected CX
Own one idea per audience — clarity beats comprehensiveness
Measure effort, not just satisfaction — CES is the strongest predictor
Speed is a CX strategy — fast everything = trust
Every interaction is a trust transaction — deposits or withdrawals

recommendedReading.title: The Effortless Experience — Dixon et al.
The Challenger Sale — Dixon & Adamson
recommendedReading.insight: Best B2B sales teaches, tailors, and takes control

recommendedReading.title: Obviously Awesome — April Dunford
recommendedReading.insight: Positioning from customer context, not features

recommendedReading.title: De-Positioning — Todd Irwin
recommendedReading.insight: Solve the pain competitors leave unresolved
